content:
# Exodus Chapter 12 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~37v–39r (PDF pp. 73–75); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 52–53 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_012_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Institution of the Passover (vv. 1–14)

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying,

**2** "This month shall be for you the beginning of months [**rosh chodashim**]; it shall be the first for you of the months of the year.

**3** Speak to all the congregation of Israel, saying: 'On the tenth of this month, let them take for themselves, each man, a lamb [**seh**] for a father's house, a lamb for a house.

**4** And if the house is too small for a lamb, then he and his neighbor who is near to his house shall take according to the number of persons [**nefashot**]; each man according to his eating you shall reckon for the lamb.

**5** A lamb, unblemished [**tamim**], a male, a year old — it shall be for you. From the sheep or from the goats you shall take it.

**6** And it shall be for you to keep [**lemishmeret**] until the fourteenth day of this month, and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall slaughter it between the evenings [**bein ha'arbayim**].

**7** And they shall take from the blood and put it on the two doorposts [**mezuzot**] and on the lintel [**mashqof**], on the houses in which they eat it.

**8** And they shall eat the flesh on that night, roasted with fire [**tseli-esh**], and unleavened bread [**matstsot**]; with bitter herbs [**merorim**] they shall eat it.

**9** Do not eat from it raw [**na**] or boiled, boiled in water [**uvashel mevushal bamayim**], but roasted with fire — its head with its legs and with its inner parts [**qirbo**].

**10** And you shall not leave any of it until morning, and what remains of it until morning you shall burn with fire.

**11** And thus shall you eat it: your loins girded, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand. And you shall eat it in haste [**bechipazon**]. It is a Passover [**pesach**] to the LORD.

**12** And I will pass through the land of Egypt on that night, and I will strike every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from man to beast; and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments [**shefatim**]. I am the LORD.

**13** And the blood shall be for you as a sign [**le'ot**] upon the houses where you are; and I will see the blood and I will pass over [**ufasachti**] you, and there shall not be among you a plague of destruction [**negef lemashchit**] when I strike in the land of Egypt.

**14** And this day shall be for you a memorial [**lezikkaron**], and you shall celebrate it as a feast to the LORD; throughout your generations, as a statute forever [**chuqqat olam**], you shall celebrate it.

## The Feast of Unleavened Bread (vv. 15–20)

**15** Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread [**matstsot**]. Indeed, on the first day you shall remove leaven [**se'or**] from your houses, for whoever eats leavened bread [**chamets**] from the first day until the seventh day — that person [**nefesh**] shall be cut off from Israel.

**16** And on the first day there shall be a holy convocation [**miqra-qodesh**], and on the seventh day there shall be a holy convocation for you. No work shall be done on them; only what is eaten by every person [**nefesh**] — that alone shall be prepared for you.

**17** And you shall keep the unleavened bread [**matstsot**], for on this very day [**be'etsem hayyom hazzeh**] I brought out your hosts from the land of Egypt. And you shall keep this day throughout your generations as a statute forever.

**18** In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, in the evening, you shall eat unleavened bread, until the twenty-first day of the month in the evening.

**19** For seven days, leaven [**se'or**] shall not be found in your houses, for whoever eats what is leavened [**machmetset**] — that person shall be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether sojourner [**ger**] or native of the land [**ezrach ha'arets**].

**20** Anything leavened you shall not eat. In all your dwelling places you shall eat unleavened bread."

## Moses Instructs the Elders (vv. 21–28)

**21** And Moses called for all the elders of Israel and said to them, "Draw out [**mishkhu**] and take for yourselves lambs according to your families, and slaughter the Passover.

**22** And you shall take a bunch of hyssop [**aguddath ezov**] and dip it in the blood that is in the basin [**saf**], and you shall touch [**vehigga'tem**] the lintel and the two doorposts with the blood that is in the basin. And you — none of you shall go out from the door of his house until morning.

**23** And the LORD will pass through to strike Egypt, and He will see the blood on the lintel and on the two doorposts, and the LORD will pass over [**ufasach**] the door, and He will not allow the destroyer [**hammashchit**] to come into your houses to strike.

**24** And you shall keep this thing as a statute for you and for your sons forever [**ad-olam**].

**25** And it shall be, when you come to the land which the LORD will give to you, as He has spoken, that you shall keep this service [**avodah**].

**26** And it shall be, when your sons say to you, 'What is this service to you?'

**27** then you shall say, 'It is the sacrifice of the Passover [**zevach-pesach**] to the LORD, who passed over [**pasach**] the houses of the sons of Israel in Egypt when He struck Egypt, and our houses He delivered [**hitstsil**].'" And the people bowed down and worshiped [**vayyishtachavu**].

**28** And the sons of Israel went and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron; so they did.

## The Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn (vv. 29–36)

**29** And it was at midnight [**bachatsi hallaylah**], and the LORD struck every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon [**beit habor**], and every firstborn of the beast.

**30** And Pharaoh rose in the night, he and all his servants and all Egypt, and there was a great cry [**tse'aqah gedolah**] in Egypt, for there was not a house where there was not one dead.

**31** And he called for Moses and Aaron at night and said, "Rise up, go out from the midst of my people, both you and the sons of Israel. And go, serve the LORD, as you have spoken.

**32** Also your flocks, also your herds — take them, as you have spoken, and go. And bless me also [**uverakhetem gam-oti**]."

**33** And Egypt was urgent upon the people, to hasten to send them out from the land, for they said, "We are all dead men [**kullanu metim**]."

**34** And the people carried their dough before it was leavened, their kneading troughs [**mish'arotam**] bound up in their garments upon their shoulders.

**35** And the sons of Israel had done according to the word of Moses, and they had asked [**vayyish'alu**] from Egypt articles of silver and articles of gold and garments.

**36** And the LORD had given the people favor [**chen**] in the eyes of Egypt, and they gave to them what they asked. And they plundered [**vaynatslu**] Egypt.

## The Departure from Egypt (vv. 37–42)

**37** And the sons of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand [**keshesh-me'ot elef**] men on foot [**ragli**], the men, besides the children [**tap**].

**38** And also a mixed multitude [**erev rav**] went up with them, and flocks and herds — very much livestock.

**39** And they baked the dough which they had brought out from Egypt into cakes of unleavened bread [**ugot matstsot**], for it had not leavened, because they were driven out from Egypt and could not delay, and also provisions they had not made for themselves.

**40** And the dwelling [**moshav**] of the sons of Israel, which they had dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years.

**41** And it was at the end of four hundred and thirty years, and it was on this very day [**be'etsem hayyom hazzeh**], all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt.

**42** It is a night of watching [**leil shimmurim**] for the LORD, to bring them out from the land of Egypt. This is that night for the LORD — a watching for all the sons of Israel throughout their generations.

## Statutes of the Passover (vv. 43–51)

**43** And the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, "This is the statute of the Passover [**chuqqat happasach**]: No foreigner [**ben-nekhar**] shall eat of it.

**44** But every man's servant purchased with silver — you shall circumcise him; then he shall eat of it.

**45** A sojourner [**toshav**] and a hired worker [**sakhir**] shall not eat of it.

**46** In one house it shall be eaten. You shall not bring any of the flesh outside the house, and a bone you shall not break in it [**ve'etsem lo tishberu-vo**].

**47** All the congregation of Israel shall observe it [**ya'asu oto**].

**48** And when a sojourner [**ger**] sojourns with you and would observe the Passover to the LORD, let every male of his be circumcised, and then he may draw near to observe it, and he shall be as a native of the land [**ke'ezrach ha'arets**]. But no uncircumcised person shall eat of it.

**49** One law [**torah achat**] shall be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns in your midst."

**50** And all the sons of Israel did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron; so they did.

**51** **And it was on this very day that the LORD brought out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt by their hosts.**

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~37v–39r (PDF pp. 73–75) contain the entirety of Exodus 12 across three pages in the characteristic three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. A prominent petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 12:1, signaling the major thematic break between the plague announcement of chapter 11 and the Passover legislation. The text of 12:1 begins with the formula vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon — notably the first time in the plague narrative that both Moses and Aaron are addressed together in a divine speech opening a major legislative section. Setumah (closed paragraph) markers appear at several internal divisions: before v. 21 (the shift from divine speech to Moses' address to the elders), before v. 29 (the execution of the plague), before v. 37 (the departure narrative), before v. 43 (the Passover statutes). A petuchah marker appears before v. 51, marking the concluding summary statement. Masorah parva annotations are visible throughout, including notes on the rare form chipazon (v. 11), on the phrase bein ha'arbayim (v. 6), and on the number six hundred thousand (v. 37). The cantillation on v. 42 (leil shimmurim) shows distinctive melodic emphasis on the doubled form shimmurim, highlighting the liturgical significance of this verse. The Codex Vaticanus pages 52–53 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 12. The text begins on page 52 (continuing from chapter 11) and extends through page 53. The most significant LXX divergence in this chapter appears at v. 40, where the Vaticanus text reads hē de katoikēsis tōn huiōn Israēl hēn katōkēsan en gē Aiguptō kai en gē Chanaan etē tetrakosia triakonta ("and the dwelling of the sons of Israel which they dwelt in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan was four hundred and thirty years") — adding "and in the land of Canaan" (kai en gē Chanaan), thereby distributing the 430 years across both the patriarchal sojourn in Canaan and the Egyptian bondage. This variant is textually significant and is discussed in detail below. The Vaticanus text also shows minor expansions at several points, particularly in the Passover regulations.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"The beginning of months" (v. 2) — rosh chodashim:** Hebrew hachodesh hazzeh lakhem rosh chodashim ri'shon hu lakhem lechodshey hashanah. This verse institutes a calendar reset. The month of the Exodus (later called Aviv/Nisan) becomes "the beginning of months" — not merely the first month in a sequence, but the head (rosh) of all months. The noun rosh ("head, beginning, chief") carries connotations of primacy and authority: this month rules over all other months. The phrase ri'shon hu lakhem ("it shall be the first for you") reinforces the personal appropriation — "for you," specifically for Israel. This calendar reform is theological, not astronomical: Egypt had its own calendar; Israel now receives a distinctly Israelite reckoning of time, anchored not in natural cycles but in the act of redemption. Time itself is reordered around the Exodus. LOGOS renders "the beginning of months" for rosh chodashim, preserving the Hebrew construct. The LXX renders archē mēnōn ("beginning of months"), matching the MT precisely.

2. **"A lamb" (v. 3) — seh:** Hebrew veyiqchu lahem ish seh leveit-avot seh labbayit. The noun seh is a general term for a small animal of the flock — either a sheep or a goat. This ambiguity is resolved in v. 5, which specifies min-hakkevashim umin-ha'izzim tiqqachu ("from the sheep or from the goats you shall take it"). The seh is thus not limited to a lamb (keves) but may be a kid (gedi). LOGOS renders "lamb" with a transliteration note, following the conventional English rendering while noting the broader semantic range. The instruction is seh leveit-avot ("a lamb for a father's house") — the patriarchal household is the basic unit of the Passover observance. The phrase seh labbayit ("a lamb for a house") then redefines the unit: not the extended clan but the individual household. The LXX renders probaton ("sheep"), narrowing the semantic range from the MT's more inclusive seh.

3. **"Between the evenings" (v. 6) — bein ha'arbayim:** Hebrew veshachat oto kol qehal adat-Yisra'el bein ha'arbayim. This temporal expression has been debated since antiquity. The phrase literally means "between the two evenings" (arbayim is a dual form of erev, "evening"). Three main interpretations exist: (a) between the decline of the sun and sunset (roughly 3:00–6:00 PM), the Pharisaic/rabbinic view; (b) between sunset and full darkness (roughly 6:00–7:30 PM), the Samaritan/Sadducean view; (c) between the evenings of two consecutive days (a less common view). The first interpretation prevailed in Second Temple practice, with the Passover lambs being slaughtered in the afternoon of the 14th of Nisan. LOGOS renders "between the evenings" to preserve the Hebrew idiom and its inherent ambiguity. The LXX renders pros esperan ("toward evening"), simplifying the dual expression.

4. **"Roasted with fire... with unleavened bread, with bitter herbs" (vv. 8–9) — the Passover meal:** Hebrew veakhlu et-habbasar ballaylah hazzeh tseli-esh umatstsot al-merorim yokheluhu. Al-tokhlu mimmennu na uvashel mevushal bamayim ki im-tseli-esh ro'sho al-kera'av ve'al-qirbo. Three preparation requirements are specified: (a) tseli-esh ("roasted with fire") — the meat must be fire-roasted, not boiled; (b) matstsot ("unleavened bread") — bread without leaven, connecting to the haste of departure; (c) merorim ("bitter herbs") — herbs of bitterness, traditionally identified with various bitter plants (lettuce, endive, horseradish), symbolizing the bitterness of Egyptian bondage. Two forbidden preparations are named: na ("raw" — though some render "underdone, half-cooked") and uvashel mevushal bamayim ("boiled, boiled in water"). The emphatic construction bashel mevushal (cognate accusative: "cooked with cooking") emphasizes the prohibition. The specification of ro'sho al-kera'av ve'al-qirbo ("its head with its legs and with its inner parts") indicates that the animal is to be roasted whole — head, legs, and entrails together — not butchered into portions. This whole-roasting preserves the integrity of the sacrificial animal. LOGOS renders each term formally. The LXX renders opta puri ("roasted with fire") and azuma epi pikridōn ("unleavened bread with bitter herbs").

5. **"In haste" (v. 11) — bechipazon:** Hebrew vekhakhah tokhlu oto motnekhem chagurim na'alekhem beragleykem umaqelkhem beyedkhem va'akhaltem oto bechipazon pesach hu laYHWH. The noun chipazon ("haste, hurry, trepidation") occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible: here, in Deuteronomy 16:3 (also referring to the Exodus), and in Isaiah 52:12 (where God says Israel will not go out in haste — a reversal of the Exodus pattern). The root ch-f-z conveys not merely speed but urgency mixed with anxiety — it is the haste of those who must flee before a window of opportunity closes. The entire description — loins girded (ready for travel), sandals on feet (prepared for a journey), staff in hand (equipped for walking) — depicts people eating while standing, dressed for immediate departure. This is not a leisurely meal but a combat-ready field ration. The declaration pesach hu laYHWH ("It is a Passover to the LORD") names the meal for the first time. The word pesach, from the verb pasach ("to pass over, to skip over, to protect"), identifies the entire complex of events — the lamb, the blood, the meal, the divine passing-over — as a single unified act called "Passover." LOGOS renders "in haste" for bechipazon and preserves the transliteration. The LXX renders meta spoudēs ("with haste/urgency").

6. **"Against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments" (v. 12) — uvekhol-elohey Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim:** Hebrew ve'avarti ve'erets-Mitsrayim ballaylah hazzeh vehikketi khol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim me'adam ve'ad-behemah uvekhol-elohey Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim ani YHWH. This verse makes explicit what has been implicit throughout the plague narrative: the plagues are not merely punishments of the Egyptian people but judgments against the Egyptian gods. The noun shefatim ("judgments, acts of judgment") comes from the root sh-f-t ("to judge"). Each plague, in retrospect, can be read as a polemic against specific Egyptian deities: the Nile god (Hapi), the frog goddess (Heqet), the sun god (Ra, in the darkness plague), and now the death of the firstborn strikes at the divinity of Pharaoh himself, whose firstborn son was considered divine. The clause ani YHWH ("I am the LORD") functions as a divine signature — a self-identification that authenticates the declaration and asserts YHWH's supreme authority over all other claimed deities. LOGOS renders "I will execute judgments" for e'eseh shefatim. The LXX renders poiēsō tēn ekdikēsin ("I will execute vengeance"), using ekdikēsis ("vengeance") rather than the more judicial "judgments."

7. **"The blood shall be for you as a sign... and I will pass over you" (v. 13) — the blood-sign and the pasach verb:** Hebrew vehayah haddam lakhem le'ot al habbattim asher attem sham vera'iti et-haddam ufasachti alekhem velo-yihyeh vakhem negef lemashchit behakkoti be'erets Mitsrayim. Two crucial elements converge: (a) The blood is le'ot ("as a sign") — the same word used for the sign of circumcision (Gen. 17:11) and the sign of the Sabbath (Exod. 31:13, 17). The blood-sign is visible, covenantal, and identity-marking. It marks the houses not merely as Israelite but as houses under divine protection. (b) The verb ufasachti ("and I will pass over") is the verb that gives the festival its name. The root pasach has been variously understood: "to pass over" (skip, spare), "to protect" (shield, hover over), or "to limp" (cf. 1 Kgs 18:21, where the same root describes the people "limping" between two opinions). The most likely meaning here is protective passing-over: God passes over the marked houses, shielding them from the negef lemashchit ("plague of destruction" or "plague belonging to the destroyer"). The noun mashchit ("destroyer") appears here as a substantive — whether this is an angelic agent, an impersonal force of destruction, or a hypostatic attribute of divine wrath has been debated. Verse 23 clarifies by distinguishing YHWH (who "passes through") from hammashchit ("the destroyer") whom YHWH will "not allow" to enter. LOGOS preserves the formal structure "pass over" for pasach and "plague of destruction" for negef lemashchit. The LXX renders skepasō humas ("I will shelter/cover you"), interpreting pasach as protective covering rather than passing-over.

8. **"Leaven" and "leavened bread" (vv. 15, 19–20) — se'or, chamets, machmetset:** Three distinct Hebrew terms appear for the concept of leaven/leavened: (a) se'or ("leaven, sourdough starter") — the leavening agent itself, the fermented dough used to cause rising; (b) chamets ("leavened bread") — bread that has undergone the process of fermentation and rising; (c) machmetset (v. 19, "what is leavened") — a Hiphil participial form, referring to anything that has been caused to become leavened. The distinction between se'or and chamets is significant: se'or is the cause (the agent), chamets is the result (the product). The command in v. 15 targets se'or — "you shall remove leaven from your houses" — while the prohibition targets chamets — "whoever eats leavened bread." The penalty of karet — being "cut off from Israel" — for eating chamets during the festival elevates the unleavened bread requirement from culinary preference to covenant obligation. LOGOS preserves the distinct terms. The LXX renders zumē ("leaven") for se'or and zumōton ("leavened") for chamets.

9. **"A bunch of hyssop" (v. 22) — aguddath ezov:** Hebrew ulqachtem aguddat ezov utvaltem baddam asher bassaf vehigga'tem el-hammashqof ve'el-shtey hammezuzot min-haddam asher bassaf. The noun aguddah ("bunch, bundle") occurs rarely in biblical Hebrew; the ezov ("hyssop") is a small aromatic plant (traditionally identified with Origanum syriacum, Syrian oregano) used in purification rituals throughout the Pentateuch (cf. Lev. 14:4, 6, 49, 51, 52; Num. 19:6, 18). Its humble size and its use for sprinkling made it the ideal instrument for blood application. The noun saf ("basin, threshold") has a double meaning: it may refer to a basin or bowl in which the blood was collected, or to the threshold of the door (the same word means "threshold" in some contexts). The ambiguity is suggestive: the blood is in the saf (basin/threshold), and it is applied to the mashqof (lintel) and mezuzot (doorposts) — all architectural elements of the doorway. The entire door-frame becomes marked with blood, turning the entrance into a boundary of protection. LOGOS renders "bunch of hyssop" and "basin" for the primary meaning. The LXX renders desmon hussōpou ("bundle of hyssop") and tou haimatos tou para tēn thuran ("the blood that is beside the door"), interpreting saf as the door-area rather than a vessel.

10. **"The LORD will pass over the door, and He will not allow the destroyer to come in" (v. 23) — ufasach YHWH... hammashchit:** Hebrew ve'avar YHWH linggof et-Mitsrayim vera'ah et-haddam al-hammashqof ve'al shtey hammezuzot ufasach YHWH al-happetach velo yitten hammashchit lavo el-batteykem linggof. This verse distinguishes two agents: (a) YHWH, who "passes through" (avar) Egypt to strike, and who "passes over" (pasach) the door; (b) hammashchit ("the destroyer"), whom YHWH "will not allow" (lo yitten, literally "will not give/permit") to enter the houses. The verb pasach here takes YHWH as subject and al-happetach ("over the doorway") as its complement — God personally stations Himself over each marked doorway as a protective presence. The mashchit is thus an agent distinct from YHWH — a destroying force or destroying angel that YHWH restrains. This is the key verse for understanding the theology of the Passover: the blood does not merely identify Israelite homes; it activates divine protection. YHWH does not merely refrain from striking; He actively prevents the destroyer from entering. The verb pasach here carries the full weight of its protective meaning: to hover over, to shield, to guard the threshold. LOGOS renders "pass over" for pasach and "the destroyer" for hammashchit. The LXX renders pareleusetai kurios tēn thuran ("the Lord will pass by the door") and ouk easei ton olethreunonta eiselthein ("He will not permit the destroyer to enter").

11. **"What is this service to you?" (vv. 26–27) — the child's question:** Hebrew vehayah ki-yomru aleykem beneykem mah ha'avodah hazzot lakhem va'amartem zevach-pesach hu laYHWH asher pasach al-battey veney-Yisra'el beMitsrayim benogpo et-Mitsrayim ve'et-batteynu hitstsil. This is the first of four questions that children will ask about Israel's rituals (cf. 13:14; Deut. 6:20; the fourth, the child who does not know how to ask, is derived from 13:8). The question mah ha'avodah hazzot lakhem ("What is this service to you?") is a genuine inquiry into meaning. The answer connects the ritual to history: zevach-pesach hu laYHWH ("It is the sacrifice of the Passover to the LORD") — identifying the Passover as a zevach ("sacrifice," from zavach, "to slaughter"), a communal meal offered to God. The verb pasach recurs as a verbal explanation of the noun pesach: the Passover is so called because God "passed over" (pasach) the houses of Israel. The response culminates in hitstsil ("He delivered"), from the Hiphil of natsal — an active verb of rescue. LOGOS renders formally, preserving the question-and-answer structure. The LXX renders thusia to pascha touto kuriō ("this sacrifice is the Passover to the Lord").

12. **"At midnight" (v. 29) — bachatsi hallaylah:** Hebrew vayhi bachatsi hallaylah vaYHWH hikkah khol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim mibbkhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashebi asher beveit habor vekhol bekhor behemah. Note the shift from 11:4 kachatsot hallaylah ("about midnight," with the approximating preposition ke-) to bachatsi hallaylah ("at midnight," with the locative preposition be-). The announcement was approximate; the execution is precise. The social spectrum also shifts: in 11:5, the lowest social class was "the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones"; here it is "the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon" (bekhor hashebi asher beveit habor, literally "the firstborn of the prisoner who was in the house of the pit"). The substitution of the prisoner for the maidservant may reflect the narrative progression — by the time of execution, the scope has expanded to include even those in the most inaccessible places (underground dungeons). LOGOS renders "at midnight" to mark the precision. The LXX renders egenēthē de en tō mesounuktiō ("and it happened at midnight").

13. **"Bless me also" (v. 32) — uverakhetem gam-oti:** Hebrew gam-tsonkhem gam-beqarkhem qechu ka'asher dibbartem valekhu uverakhetem gam-oti. Pharaoh's final words to Moses contain this stunning request: "And bless me also." After ten plagues, the death of his firstborn, and total capitulation, Pharaoh asks for a blessing from the very man he had repeatedly dismissed. The verb barekh (Piel of barakh) means "to bless" — to invoke or transmit divine favor. Pharaoh, having witnessed that YHWH's power surpasses all Egyptian gods, now seeks YHWH's blessing through YHWH's servant. The particle gam ("also") is poignant — bless me too, along with yourselves. This is the ultimate reversal of the Exodus narrative's power dynamic. LOGOS renders literally. The LXX renders eulogēsate de kame ("and bless me also").

14. **"They plundered Egypt" (v. 36) — vaynatslu et-Mitsrayim:** Hebrew vaYHWH natan et-chen ha'am be'einey Mitsrayim vayyash'ilum vaynatslu et-Mitsrayim. The verb natslu (Piel of natsal, here meaning "to strip, to plunder, to despoil") is striking. In its Hiphil form (hitstsil), this verb means "to deliver, to rescue" (cf. v. 27). In its Piel form, it means "to strip, to despoil." The same root that describes God's deliverance of Israel also describes Israel's despoliation of Egypt. The wordplay is deliberate: the delivered (natsal Hiphil) despoil (natsal Piel). Egypt, which had enslaved and exploited Israel for generations, is now stripped of its wealth. This fulfills the promise of Genesis 15:14: "they shall come out with great possessions." LOGOS renders "plundered" for vaynatslu. The LXX renders eskuleasan ("they despoiled/plundered").

15. **"Six hundred thousand men on foot" (v. 37) — keshesh-me'ot elef ragli:** Hebrew vayyis'u veney-Yisra'el meRa'amses Sukkothah keshesh-me'ot elef ragli haggevarim levad mittaf. The number six hundred thousand (shesh-me'ot elef, literally "six hundred thousand") refers to men on foot (ragli, "footmen"), further specified as haggevarim ("the men," i.e., adult males), levad mittaf ("besides the children"). This number, which would imply a total population of 2–3 million including women and children, has been the subject of extensive scholarly discussion. The Hebrew word elef can mean "thousand" or "clan/military unit" — some scholars propose reading "six hundred clans" rather than "six hundred thousand individuals." LOGOS renders the number at face value, as the formal reading of the MT demands. The LXX renders eis hexakosias chiliadas pezōn ("about six hundred thousand on foot"), confirming the traditional numerical reading.

16. **"A mixed multitude" (v. 38) — erev rav:** Hebrew vegam-erev rav alah ittam vetson uvaqar miqneh kaved me'od. The phrase erev rav ("a great mixed multitude") refers to non-Israelites who joined the Exodus. The noun erev (from the root '-r-v, "to mix, to mingle") denotes a mixture — a heterogeneous group of various ethnic and social backgrounds. These may have included other Semitic slaves, Egyptian sympathizers, and various displaced peoples who saw in Israel's departure an opportunity for their own liberation. The presence of the erev rav complicates the narrative's ethnic categories: the Exodus is not exclusively an Israelite event but includes others who attached themselves to Israel's God and Israel's fate. Later tradition views the erev rav ambivalently — they are sometimes blamed for the golden calf incident (32:4) and other failures. LOGOS renders "a mixed multitude" for erev rav. The LXX renders kai epimiktos polus ("and a great mixed company").

17. **"Four hundred and thirty years" (v. 40) — the LXX variant:** Hebrew umoshav beney Yisra'el asher yashvu beMitsrayim sheloshim shanah ve'arba me'ot shanah. The MT states that "the dwelling of the sons of Israel, which they had dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years." The LXX (Vaticanus), however, reads: hē de katoikēsis tōn huiōn Israēl hēn katōkēsan en gē Aiguptō kai en gē Chanaan etē tetrakosia triakonta ("the dwelling of the sons of Israel which they dwelt in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan was four hundred and thirty years"). The addition of kai en gē Chanaan ("and in the land of Canaan") distributes the 430 years across both the patriarchal period in Canaan and the time in Egypt. This LXX reading is supported by the Samaritan Pentateuch and was known to Paul (Gal. 3:17, where the 430 years runs from the Abrahamic covenant to Sinai). If the 430 years covers both Canaan and Egypt, the actual time in Egypt would be approximately 215 years (from Jacob's descent to the Exodus). If the MT's reading is taken strictly, the entire 430 years was spent in Egypt. LOGOS follows the MT reading. The LXX variant is noted for its textual and theological significance.

18. **"A night of watching" (v. 42) — leil shimmurim:** Hebrew leil shimmurim hu laYHWH lehotsi'am me'erets Mitsrayim hu-hallaylah hazzeh laYHWH shimmurim lekhol-beney Yisra'el ledorotam. The noun shimmurim is a plural/intensive form of shimmur ("watching, keeping, vigil"), from the root sh-m-r ("to guard, to keep, to watch over"). The phrase leil shimmurim hu laYHWH ("It is a night of watching for the LORD") can be understood in two directions: (a) it is a night on which the LORD kept watch — God was vigilant through the night, watching over Israel and executing judgment on Egypt; (b) it is a night of watching in honor of the LORD — Israel keeps vigil in commemoration of God's deliverance. Both meanings are likely intended. The repetition shimmurim lekhol-beney Yisra'el ledorotam ("a watching for all the sons of Israel throughout their generations") establishes perpetual observance: every generation reenacts the vigil. LOGOS renders "a night of watching" for leil shimmurim. The LXX renders profulakē estin tō kuriō ("it is a keeping-watch for the Lord"), using profulakē ("advance guard, vigil").

19. **"A bone you shall not break in it" (v. 46) — ve'etsem lo tishberu-vo:** Hebrew bevayit echad ye'akhel lo-totsi min-habbayit min-habbasar chutsah ve'etsem lo tishberu-vo. Three regulations for the Passover lamb: (a) it must be eaten in one house (not distributed among multiple locations); (b) no flesh may be taken outside the house; (c) no bone may be broken. The prohibition against breaking a bone (etsem, "bone") has both practical and symbolic dimensions. Practically, unbroken bones facilitate whole-roasting (cf. v. 9). Symbolically, the intact skeleton represents the wholeness and integrity of the sacrifice — the Passover lamb is offered whole to God. The New Testament identifies this regulation as typologically fulfilled in Jesus' crucifixion: "Not one of his bones will be broken" (John 19:36, citing this verse and/or Ps. 34:20). The LXX renders ostoun ou suntripsete ap' autou ("a bone you shall not crush/break from it"), using suntribō ("to crush, to shatter"), the same verb quoted in John 19:36.

20. **"One law" (v. 49) — torah achat:** Hebrew torah achat yihyeh la'ezrach velaggeir haggar betokhekhem. The phrase torah achat ("one law, a single instruction") establishes legal equality between the native Israelite (ezrach, "native-born, one who has sprung up from the land") and the sojourner (ger, "resident alien, immigrant"). This principle of legal unity — one law for all — is foundational for Israel's jurisprudence and recurs throughout the Pentateuch (cf. Lev. 24:22; Num. 9:14; 15:15–16, 29). The ger who is circumcised and observes the Passover has the same legal standing as the native Israelite. LOGOS renders formally. The LXX renders nomos heis estai tō engchōriō kai tō prosēlutō ("one law shall be for the native and for the proselyte"), rendering ger as prosēlutos — the word that becomes the standard term for a convert to Judaism.

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 12

*Note: Exodus 12 contains no hardening-verb instances. The last hardening formula was 11:10 ("the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"). The next hardening will occur in 14:4 ("I will strengthen the heart of Pharaoh"), when Pharaoh pursues the departed Israelites to the Sea of Reeds. The entire Passover-departure narrative of chapters 12–13 is thus bracketed by divine hardenings — the hardening that prevented release (11:10) and the hardening that drives pursuit (14:4). In between, the Passover narrative unfolds without any mention of Pharaoh's heart: the focus shifts from Pharaoh's will to God's action.*

### Theological Note: The Architecture of Exodus 12

Exodus 12 is the theological center of the entire Pentateuch — the chapter where Israel's founding event is simultaneously narrated and legislated. No other chapter in Scripture so completely interweaves historical event with ritual instruction: the institution of the Passover (vv. 1–14), the feast of unleavened bread (vv. 15–20), Moses' instruction to the elders (vv. 21–28), the execution of the tenth plague (vv. 29–36), the departure (vv. 37–42), and the statutes governing future observance (vv. 43–51). The chapter moves fluidly between divine command and narrative fulfillment, between "thus shall you do" and "thus they did."

The chapter's structure reveals a deliberate chiastic pattern centered on the execution of the plague. The outer frames (vv. 1–14 and vv. 43–51) contain divine legislation for the Passover. The intermediate frames (vv. 15–20 and vv. 37–42) concern unleavened bread and departure. The inner frames (vv. 21–28 and vv. 29–36) narrate instruction and fulfillment — Moses tells the elders what to do, and then the event occurs. At the center stands the midnight strike (v. 29), the hinge on which the entire Exodus turns.

Three theological themes dominate the chapter:

**First, the reordering of time (v. 2).** The declaration that the Exodus month is "the beginning of months" is not merely a calendrical adjustment but a theological revolution. Israel's time will henceforth be measured not from creation (as in the primeval history) or from agricultural cycles (as in Canaanite religion) but from redemption. The first month of the Israelite year is the month of liberation. Every subsequent date in Israel's calendar is reckoned from this point. Time itself becomes a witness to the Exodus.

**Second, the blood-protection theology (vv. 7, 13, 22–23).** The blood of the Passover lamb, applied to the doorposts and lintel, creates a zone of divine protection around each household. The blood does not atone (there is no confession of sin, no laying of hands, no priestly mediation); rather, it identifies and protects. The three application points — two doorposts and the lintel — frame the doorway completely. Anyone inside the blood-marked house is safe; anyone outside is exposed. The blood divides the world into two spaces: inside (life, safety, the presence of God who "passes over" the door) and outside (death, the destroyer, the plague that strikes Egypt). This spatial theology of the blood anticipates the later tabernacle/temple theology where the blood-sprinkled holy of holies is the ultimate "inside" — the place of maximum divine protection.

**Third, the memorial obligation (vv. 14, 24–27, 42).** The Passover is not merely a historical event but a perpetual institution. The command lezikkaron ("as a memorial") and the child's question ("What is this service to you?") establish the mechanism of intergenerational transmission. Each generation must not merely remember the Exodus intellectually but reenact it ritually — eating the same meal, in the same haste, with the same unleavened bread and bitter herbs. The phrase chuqqat olam ("a statute forever") makes the Passover a permanent feature of Israel's covenant life. The night of watching (leil shimmurim, v. 42) is both a divine act (God watching over Israel on that first night) and a human obligation (Israel keeping vigil in every subsequent generation). The watching is mutual: God watched for Israel, and Israel watches for God.

The chapter also reveals the inclusivity-within-boundaries that characterizes Israelite covenant theology. The erev rav ("mixed multitude," v. 38) joins the Exodus, and the statutes of vv. 43–49 establish the mechanism by which non-Israelites may participate in the Passover: circumcision is the boundary marker, and once circumcised, the ger has the same standing as the ezrach. The remarkable declaration of torah achat ("one law," v. 49) — one law for the native and for the sojourner — establishes a principle of legal equality that will reverberate through the entire Pentateuch and beyond.

Finally, the unbroken bone of v. 46 reaches forward through the canon. The wholeness of the Passover lamb — roasted intact, consumed entirely, with no bone broken — is a symbol of the integrity of the sacrifice. The New Testament identifies Jesus as "the Lamb of God" (John 1:29) and his crucifixion as the fulfillment of the Passover typology, explicitly citing the unbroken-bone provision: "These things happened so that the Scripture might be fulfilled: 'Not a bone of him shall be broken'" (John 19:36). The entire Passover complex of Exodus 12 — the lamb, the blood, the haste, the deliverance, the memorial meal — becomes in Christian reading the typological foundation for the theology of the cross and the Eucharist.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 12 (pages 52–53) preserves the LXX version with several notable divergences from the MT. The most significant is the expansion at v. 40, where the LXX adds kai en gē Chanaan ("and in the land of Canaan") to the description of the Israelites' sojourn, distributing the 430 years across both Canaan and Egypt. This variant is also found in the Samaritan Pentateuch and was known in early Judaism (cf. the Book of Jubilees, which calculates the Egyptian sojourn as approximately 210 years). The Vaticanus text at v. 13 reads skepasō humas ("I will shelter you") rather than "I will pass over you," interpreting the verb pasach as protective covering — a reading that emphasizes the shielding aspect of God's action. At v. 23, the LXX reads pareleusetai kurios tēn thuran ("the Lord will pass by the door"), using parerchomai ("to pass by") rather than a cognate of pasach, and ton olethreunonta ("the destroyer"), using the present participle of olothreuō — a term that became standard in later Jewish and Christian references to the destroying angel (cf. 1 Cor. 10:10, ho olothreutēs, "the destroyer"). At v. 35, the LXX adds himatismon ("clothing/garments") alongside the silver and gold, matching the MT's reference to semalot ("garments"). At v. 37, the LXX reads eis hexakosias chiliadas pezōn hoi andres ("about six hundred thousand on foot, the men"), confirming the numerical reading. At v. 42, the LXX reads profulakē estin tō kuriō ("it is a vigil/watch for the Lord"), using profulakē, a military term for an advance guard or watch — God's night-watch over Israel is depicted in military guarding language. At v. 46, the LXX reads ostoun ou suntripsete ap' autou ("a bone you shall not crush from it"), using the verb suntribō that is cited verbatim in John 19:36. Throughout, the Vaticanus text maintains close fidelity to the MT's structure while showing the characteristic LXX tendencies toward clarification and occasional theological interpretation.

--- LOGOS
